coredata.dc:title: N,S-GQDs mixed with CdTe quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescence visual detection and quantitative analysis of malachite green in fish
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Jingyao
Na, Lihua
Li, Yuanming
Bai, Wenfeng
Zhang, Jianpo
Jin, Li
coredata.dc:description: N,S-GQDS was prepared through citric acid cracking method from thiourea and citric acid, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of malachite green based on CdTe quantum dots and N,S-GQDs. Interestingly the color of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could change from red to blue under UV light upon exposure to different amounts of malachite green, and this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for visual detection of malachite green. Fluorescence emission spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different amounts of malachite green were also analyzed, and there was a good linear relationship between F460nm/F666nm and concentrations of malachite green, the lowest limit detection could reach 0.4597 nmol/L. Furthermore, this proposed ratiometric sensor is used for quantitative analysis of malachite green in fish sample. Results shown that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor is feasibility and practicality for both visual detection and quantitative analysis of malachite green.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-GQDs
CdTe quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Malachite green
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectrum (a) and TEM image of NSG (b).

The fluorescence emission spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor upon the addition of (a) 0 µM MG; (b) 8.22 µM MG; (c) 16.44 µM MG. Insert: corresponding fluorescence color under a 365 nm UV lamp.
Fluorescence spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different concentrations of MG, fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG and QDs were 0.5809 (A), 1.031(C) and 1.529 (E).
The responses of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor, NSG and QDs to 3 mg/L of different interfering substances.
Statistical information from Fig. S8 and Fig. 3.
The analytical results for MG in fish sample of Songhua Lake.

head.ce:title: N,S-GQDs mixed with CdTe quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescence visual detection and quantitative analysis of malachite green in fish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin 132022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology, Jilin 132022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics, Qingdao University of Science Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry of Education/Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Rubber-Plastics, Qingdao University of Science Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N,S-GQDS was prepared through citric acid cracking method from thiourea and citric acid, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of malachite green based on CdTe quantum dots and N,S-GQDs. Interestingly the color of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could change from red to blue under UV light upon exposure to different amounts of malachite green, and this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for visual detection of malachite green. Fluorescence emission spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor with different amounts of malachite green were also analyzed, and there was a good linear relationship between F460nm/F666nm and concentrations of malachite green, the lowest limit detection could reach 0.4597 nmol/L. Furthermore, this proposed ratiometric sensor is used for quantitative analysis of malachite green in fish sample. Results shown that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor is feasibility and practicality for both visual detection and quantitative analysis of malachite green.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (named MG in short), as an effective fungicide and parasiticide, could cure the water mildew of fish bodies to prolong the life of scale damaged fish, which has been widely used in aquaculture and transportation. But as a toxic triphenylmethane compound, MG could raise the risk of carcinogenicity and teratogenicity to human body, so MG was limited or prohibited by many countries (Tian et al., 2019). However, there is no other special drug on the market that can cure the water mildew as effective as MG in a short time, MG fails to be prohibited, especially in the process of transportation (Hu et al., 2020).

Different detection methods are used to quantitative analysis MG in recent years, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chen & Shui, 2010; Chen et al., 2020), surface enhanced Raman (SERS) (Kim et al., 2021), spray-mass spectrometry (Wei et al., 2017), electrochemical method (Li & Wu, 2021), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Kim et al., 2021) and fluorescence method (Han et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2019). Among them, fluorescence quenching method have advantages of fast response, superior sensitivity, simple operation and et al, which has been widely applied on quantitative analysis. Hu (Hu et al., 2020) first constructed fluorescence sensor for MG detection using Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots. However single wavelength fluorescence quenching method suffer some factors, such as surrounding environment, light source intensity noise, probe concentration and et al (Qu et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2015).

Ratiometric fluorescence sensor with two different wavelengths as signal and reference channel, could effectively overcome these factors (Shangguan et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2019), which can provide a built-in correction of the background interference, leading to improved signal to noise ratio measurement, and better detection sensitivity and accuracy (Yang et al., 2017a; Edwardson et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019). There were two fluorescence emission peak from single or two fluorescence materials in ratiometric fluorescence sensor system. The traditional fluorescence materials include organic dyes, fluorescent nanomaterials, lanthanide ions, and Au NCs (Lee et al., 2017; Ganganboina et al., 2017; zhao et al., 2018; Li et al., 2021). Luo and Li (Yan et al., 2018) have applied ratiometric fluorescence method for MG detection based on dual-emission BSA-protected gold nanoclusters, but the low detection limit for MG was 0.19 μM, should be further improved.

In this paper, N,S-GQDs (named NSG in short) was synthesized through citric acid cracking method from thiourea and citric acid, of which fluorescence emission wavelength of was at 460 nm (show blue under UV light). CdTe quantum dots (named QDs in short) with the characteristic of single-excitation/multiple-emissions was synthesized as we have reported, of which fluorescence emission wavelength was at 666 nm (show red under UV light). Because both the fluorescence emission of NSG and QDs could be quenched by MG, but quenching rate of NSG and QDs adopted by MG was different, the color of NSG and QDs mixture system will change, when enough MG was introduced. Moreover there were a good line relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs (F460nm/666nm) and the concentrations of MG introduced. A ratiometric method for visual detection and quantitative analysis of MG was constructed which could be applied to detect MG analyte.

3-Mercaptopropyl acid (MPA) (99+%), tellurium powder (∼200 mesh, 99.8%), CdCl2 (99+%), NaBH4 (99%) were from Aldrich Chemical Co. Malachite green was purchased from Tianjin hongyan Fine Chemical Industry Research Institute. Citric acid and thiourea were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All other agents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Water used throughout was doubly distilled water (>18 MΩ·cm).

The fluorescence spectra were measured on F-280 spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp and quartz carrier at room temperature with excitation and emission slit at 5.0 nm. Fluorescence decay curve was obtained using FLS 920 transient steady state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments). The transmission electron micrographs were recorded with a Tecnai G220 electron microscope. The XPS spectra were recorded with a Scientific K-Alpha. Laman spectrum was recorded with a BWS465-532S. The fluorescence photographs were recorded using Huawei mate 30 telephone.

QDs were prepared in the aqueous phase by two reaction steps according to the method we have reported (Zhang et al., 2017). NSG power was prepared by a modified method (Dong et al., 2012, 2021; He et al., 2014). 0.4 g citric acid and thiourea mixture (mass ratio is 1:1) added into 50 flask was heated directly for two minutes on the electric stove, until turn to light yellow, then 10 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.3 mol/L) was added into the flask under ultrasonic. As-prepared solution was condensed by rotary evaporation, and then dried under 80 ℃ for 2 h, light yellow power was obtained.

Crucian, a kind of edible fish, was bought from the local market near Songhua Lake. Crucian was placed in 40 ℃ warm water, and dead after 5 min. Then this dead fish was put on the anatomical disc, of which scale and meat were taken for experiment. Based on the fact that fish bought from the market was alive, but the fish were euthanized and procured deceased, which meets the requirements of the ethical review of our college, so the review agrees to carry out the relevant experiment (Permit No. 20190009, Jilin, China). Following Ref (Hu et al., 2020), crucian scales and meat were pretreated with some modification. To do this, fish scales (3 g) and finely chopped meat (3 g) were put into 100 mL beaker with 10 mL phosphate buffer, respectively. After ultrasonic for 1 min, supernatant liquid was collected after 5 min centrifugation at 3000 rpm.

Then 20 μL supernatant was added into NSG and QDs solution (100 mM, phosphate buffer, pH 9), and different concentrations of MG was added into this solution, the final volume was 2 mL. After incubating for 2 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (excitation wavelength was 380 nm, fluorescence emission wavelength were 460 and 666 nm).

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of NSG is at ∼ 460 nm (λex = 380 nm), blue color could be observed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1 insert). TEM image (Fig. 1B) shows that the NSG were nearly spherical in shape with a well dispersion, and the inter-planer distance of crystalline lattice plane is 0.33 nm, which is a little more than that of GQDs (He et al., 2015). Raman spectrum was shown in Fig. S1, two characteristic peaks of NSG, corresponded to D band and G band (Jian et al., 2016), were at ∼ 1408 cm−1 and ∼ 1640 cm−1 respectively, which were the characteristic peaks of graphene. Band intensity ID/IG (0.9301) is more that of graphene (0.5832), indicated that the surface defects on the graphene increased after adopted with nitrogen and sulfur.

As shown in Fig. S2, based on the fluorescence decay curve, weighted average method was used to calculate the average fluorescence lifetime of NSG (5.05 ns), which was obviously smaller than that of GQDs (6.14 ns) (Zhang et al., 2016). This because that fluorescence lifetime was corresponding to surface detects, reduction energy level decreased with the decreasing of surface defects, induced the increasing of fluorescence lifetime, which was consistent with result of Raman spectrum. Also due to the decreasing of surface defects, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of NSG (460 nm) was blue shift, compared with that of GQDs (480 nm).

Furthermore, XPS spectra (Fig. S3) were used to analysis the elements and surface groups. As shown in Fig. S3A, characteristic peaks of O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p were at 531, 399, 288 and 163 eV, and of which percentage content were 32.9 %, 12.94 %, 48.53 % and 5.62 %, respectively. There were five fitting peaks in deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S3B), corresponded to CC/CC (284.5 eV), 1S (285 eV), CN (285.6 eV), CO/CN (287.9 eV), COOH (288.9 eV). There were three fitting peaks in Fig. S3C, corresponded to pyridinic nitrogen (398.5 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (399.5 eV), graphite type nitrogen (400.5 eV) and NH (401.6 eV). There were four fitting peaks in Fig. S3D, corresponded to S H (161.8 eV), 2p3 (163.9 eV) and 2p1 (165.1 eV). All above indicated that NSG has been successfully synthesized and there were hydroxyl, carboxyl, mercapto and amino group on its surface.

This ratiometric fluorescence sensor was formed through mixing NSG with QDs, the fluorescence spectra of single NSG, single QDs and this ratiometric fluorescence sensor were shown in Fig. S4. The maximum fluorescence intensity of NSG (at 460 nm) and QDs (at 666 nm) were measured to be 808.75 and 770.20, respectively. After mixed, the fluorescence spectrum of NSG and QDs became a camelback shaped without overlapping, fluorescence intensity at 460 nm and 666 nm were 729.15 and 761.59, respectively. The intensity ratio of NSG and QDs (F460 nm/F666 nm) changed from 1.05 to 1.04 before and after mixing, scarcely no changed in F460 nm/666 nm was found, which is an essential prerequisite for design of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

In order to study the interaction between NSG and QDs, different concentrations of QDs was added into a certain concentration NSG solution. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of NSG decreased with increasing of concentrations of QDs, and there were a good linear relationship between variety of fluorescence intensity of NSG (F0/F) and concentration of QDs. The slope of the regression line decreased, as the incubation temperature increased from 20 ℃ to 30 ℃, as shown in Fig. S6, which is characteristic of static quenching progress from the quenching mechanism of Jingou Xu (Zhang et al., 2016), indicated that a composite was formed between NSG and QDs. It is mainly due to sulfhydryl group on the surface of NSG could react with Cd on the surface of QDs. The stability of this NSG-QDs composite decreased when incubation temperature increased, so the slope of regression line also decreased. This is also consistent with the result of fluorescence spectrum in Fig. S4, hump spectrum of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor indicated formation of composite from NSG and QDs, otherwise spectrum of NSG and QDs will overlap with each other. Above all a ratiometric fluorescence sensor from QDs and NSG was successfully constructed.

MG was selected as a candidate to evaluate detection performance of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor. For best results, effect of pH and incubation time on this ratiometric fluorescence sensor were firstly adopted, as shown in Fig. S7A. In this ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the concentration of QDs and NSG were 0.2119 mg/L and 40 mg/L, and the concentration of MG added was 5 mg/L. Results shown that F 460 nm/F 666 nm of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor increased firstly and then decreased along with the increasing of pH, and pH = 9 was selected for further study. From Fig. S7B, F 460 nm/F 666 nm of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor scarcely has no change, 2 min was selected as bathing time for further study.

As shown in Fig. 2 , because red emission (fluorescence emission of QDs) has greater penetration, although the concentration of NSG was obviously higher than the concentration of QDs, when fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs was about 2.876, the color of this ratiometric sensor system still showed red under UV light. Interestingly the color of this solution changed from red to purple after 8.22 µM MG was introduced, then changed to blue after 16.44 µM MG was introduced, which shown that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for visual detection of MG, distinguished by independent observers.

While different fluorescence intensity ratio maybe affect the fluorescence properties of this ratiometric sensor system, three fluorescence intensity ratio of QDs and NSG (0.5809, 1.031and 1.529) were selected for further studies, as shown in Fig. 3 . When different concentration of MG (MG) was added into three ratiometric sensor system (A, C and E), respectively, both the fluorescence intensity of QDs and NSG were decreasing gradually. There was a good linear relationship between the concentrations of MG and F0/F (the variety of the fluorescence intensity of NSG or QDs), in which F0 and F corresponded to the fluorescence intensity of NSG or QDs without and with MG. Linear regression analysis has been applied to analysis variety of fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. S8. The equation LOD= (3.3σ/k) was used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD), where σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercepts of the regression lines and k is the slope of the regression lines (Gore et al., 2014). In order to facilitate the analysis, all statistical information from Fig. S8 were shown in Table 1 , K1 and LOD1 was the slope of the linear regression line and lowest limit detection from quenching of NSG induced by MG, while K2 and LOD2 was the slope of the linear regression line and lowest limit detection from quenching of QDs induced by MG. As quenching rate was direct proportion to the slop of regression line, obviously k2 was bigger than k1, namely the quenching rate of QDs induced by MG faster than that of NSG, so the fluorescence intensity variety of QDs induced by MG (Red under UV light) decreased faster than that of NSG (blue under UV light).

This could be used to explain the color change of this ratiometric sensor system under UV light in Fig. 2. After MG was introduced into this ratiometric sensor system, red emission was gradually quenched, and blue emission (fluorescence emission of NSG) was slow to emerge. In order to make the color change more sensitive, the concentration of NSG must obviously be higher than QDs, otherwise there will be no visual color change observed under UV light.

As shown in Fig. 3B, D and F, there were a good linear relationship between the concentration of MG and F460 nm/F666 nm (fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs), the slope of the linear regression line (K3) and lowest limit detection (LOD3) were shown in Table 1 too. LOD3 (the lowest detection limit of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor) was significant lower than LOD1 (single NSG) and LOD2 (single QDs). Moreover R2 of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor also has improved compared with that of single NSG and QDs. As LOD were used to evaluate sensitivity of detection method, the lower LOD, the more sensitivity of this method, and R2 were used to evaluate and the quality of regression model, the larger R2 (close to 1), the better the fitted regression equation. So this ratiometric fluorescence sensor has significant advantage compared with single NSG or QDs. The reason could be explain as below. For single-signal fluorescence detection, sample background noise often affected the detection (Yang et al., 2017b). While for ratiometric fluorescent sensor, there were two fluorescence peaks at different wavelengths, the quantitative analysis of sample was adopted base on variety of intensity ratio of two fluorescence emission peaks. Whether both of fluorescence emission peaks decreased or one of them decreased, a built-in correction of the background interference can be achieved, which could significantly improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection.

Above all, we concluded that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for MG detection. For different requirements, different fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs was selected. In order to observe obvious color changes under UV light, fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs about 2.876 was selected for visual detection of MG, while in order to achieve lower limit detection, fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG to QDs about 0.5809 was selected for spectrum analysis of MG. As shown in Fig. S9, when fluorescence intensity of NSG and QDs was about 0.5809, the lowest limit detection could reach 0.4597 nmol/L, the linear range was 1.37–13.7 nmol/L, the regression equation was y = 0.02519x + 1.149, R2 = 0.9971. While the lowest detection limit of single NSG or QDs in same concentration were 4.830 nmol/L and 0.2891 μmol/L, respectively. Also compared with other fluorescence method (Table S1), this ratiometric fluorescence probes method has lower detection limit, although its detection range a little narrow, we could meet the test requirements by dilution of samples.

The selectivity plays a significant role in evaluating performances of sensors. The responses of single NSG, QDs and this ratiometric fluorescence sensor to some dyes, amino acids and metal ions has investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 . Dyes selected included Ponceau 4R (PR), amaranth (Am) and methyl violet (MV), amino acids selected included l-cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), trypsinogen (Try) and histidine (His), metal ions selected included Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+ and Fe2+. The fluorescence intensity change of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor was smaller than that of NSG and QDs, indicated that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor has clear superiority in selectivity compared with NSG and QDs. And only Cu2+ shows remarkable fluorescence signal change, but the concentrations of Cu2+ in real samples were too low to cause interference, which exhibited good selectivity of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

The stability of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor has been investigated. It was well known that fluorescence intensity of single NSG and QDs may change a little under continuous light excitation and at high ionic strength, but there was no effect on the intensity ratio F460 nm/F666 nm (as shown in Fig. S10), which indicated good photostability and feasibility for application.

Recovery is another important indicator to evaluate performances of sensors, and experiments of standard addition was adopted. Fish sample obtained from Songhua Lake was tested, no obvious fluorescence spectra change has been detected, indicating there was no MG or the concentration of MG was too low to be detected. Then scales and meat sample spiked with different concentrations of MG was tested using this ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Results in Table 2 shown that the recoveries ranges was 93.05 % − 107.2 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4.8 %, such results are quite close to those obtained from HPLC, indicated that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be used for quantitative analysis of fish samples.

We demonstrated here a method for synthesis of NSG, which has been used to construct a dual-emission ratiometric sensor (NSG along with QDs) for quantitative and visual detection of MG. The presence of 16.44 μM MG in solution could induce color change (from red to blue) of this ratiometric sensor, and quantitative analysis of MG could also be realized through measurement of fluorescence intensity change (F460 nm/666 nm, the fluorescence intensity ratio of NSG at 460 nm to QDs at 666 nm), and the lowest limit detection could reach 0.4597 nmol/L. Compared with single NSG and QDs system, the sensitivity of this ratiometric sensor also has be improved. Moreover this ratiometric sensor was applied to detect MG in fish sample, of which recoveries was 93.05 % − 107.2 %. All results indicated that this ratiometric sensor is applicable.
TEM and XRD data were obtained using equipment maintained by Jilin Institute of Chemical Technology Center of Characterization and Analysis”.

Jingyao Qiu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Lihua Na: Validation, Data curation. Yuanming Li: Investigation. Wenfeng Bai: Investigation, Data curation. Jianpo Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Li Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
